verses.text: When you sit to eat with a ruler, consider carefully what is before you, and 'be not desirous of his dainties: for they are deceitful meat.' Curb appetite at the great man's table. The luxuries of the powerful often carry hidden cost.

When you sit to eat with a ruler, consider carefully what is before you, and 'be not desirous of his dainties: for they are deceitful meat.' Curb appetite at the great man's table. The luxuries of the powerful often carry hidden cost.

When you sit to eat with a ruler, consider carefully what is before you, and 'be not desirous of his dainties: for they are deceitful meat.' Curb appetite at the great man's table. The luxuries of the powerful often carry hidden cost.
'Labour not to be rich,' and cease from your own wisdom; riches make wings and fly away like an eagle. Wealth is fleeting. To wear oneself out chasing money is to grasp at what soon vanishes.
'Labour not to be rich,' and cease from your own wisdom; riches make wings and fly away like an eagle. Wealth is fleeting. To wear oneself out chasing money is to grasp at what soon vanishes.

Eat not the bread of the stingy, whose heart is not with you though his mouth invites; the morsel will turn sour, and your kind words be wasted. Beware false hospitality. Grudging gifts bring no real fellowship.

Speak not in the ears of a fool, for he will despise your wisdom; remove not the old landmark nor enter the fatherless's fields, 'for their redeemer is mighty.' God defends the orphan. To wrong the helpless is to face their powerful Redeemer.

Apply your heart to instruction; withhold not correction from a child, for the rod will not kill but deliver his soul from hell. Discipline is mercy. Loving correction saves a child from a worse end.

'My son, if thine heart be wise, my heart shall rejoice'; envy not sinners, but be in the fear of the LORD all day, 'for surely there is an end; and thine expectation shall not be cut off.' A wise child gladdens a father. Reverence has a sure and lasting reward.

Hear and be wise, guiding your heart in the way; be not among winebibbers or gluttons, 'for the drunkard and the glutton shall come to poverty.' Excess impoverishes. Self-indulgence ends in want and drowsy ruin.

Hearken to your father and despise not your mother when she is old; 'buy the truth, and sell it not'; the father of the righteous shall greatly rejoice. Honour your parents. Get truth at any price, and never trade it away.

'My son, give me thine heart,' and let your eyes observe my ways; a harlot is a deep ditch, and a strange woman a narrow pit, lying in wait for prey. God asks the heart. The seductress is a trap set for the unwary.

Who has woe, sorrow, and redness of eyes? They that tarry long at the wine; look not on its sparkle, for 'at the last it biteth like a serpent, and stingeth like an adder.' Drink deceives. Its bright promise ends in venom and grief.

Who has woe, sorrow, and redness of eyes? They that tarry long at the wine; look not on its sparkle, for 'at the last it biteth like a serpent, and stingeth like an adder.' Drink deceives. Its bright promise ends in venom and grief.

Wine makes the eyes see strange things and the heart speak perverse words; the drunkard is like one lying in the sea, beaten yet unaware, craving more. Drunkenness enslaves. It dulls the senses and binds a man to seek it again.
